title: Introduction
description: Official Talonic MCP server for AI agents. Nine tools and two resources for structured document extraction via the Model Context Protocol.

content.text: The `@talonic/mcp` package is the official Talonic MCP server. It gives AI agents nine tools and two resources for extracting structured, schema-validated data from any document via the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io).

When an agent needs to pull structured data out of a PDF, scan, image, or messy document, the usual approach is raw OCR plus an LLM call. Results are unreliable; tables get mangled, dates get misread, totals drift.

With this MCP server installed, the agent has a `talonic_extract` tool that returns schema-validated JSON with per-field confidence scores, a detected document type, and stable IDs for follow-up calls. The other eight tools cover the rest of the workflow: requesting a browser upload link for files too large to pass through a hosted connector, searching the workspace, filtering by extracted field values, fetching document metadata, getting OCR markdown, listing saved schemas, saving new ones, and reading the workspace credit balance for budget-aware behaviour.

Listed on [Cursor Directory](https://cursor.directory/plugins/talonic), [Smithery](https://smithery.ai/servers/talonic/talonic), the [official MCP Registry](https://registry.modelcontextprotocol.io/) as `io.github.talonicdev/talonic-mcp`, [mcp.so](https://mcp.so/server/talonic-mcp), and [Glama](https://glama.ai/mcp/servers/talonicdev/talonic-mcp).

faq.question: What is the Talonic MCP server?
faq.answer: An official Model Context Protocol server that gives AI agents nine tools for document extraction, search, filtering, schema management, and credit-balance lookup via the Talonic API.

title: Installation
seoTitle: Install Talonic MCP Server — Talonic Docs
description: Three install paths: Claude.ai connector via OAuth (recommended), local stdio via npx for IDE clients, or hosted with a Bearer header for custom-header clients.

content.text: Three install paths. Pick the one that matches your client.

The hosted MCP endpoint accepts both `https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp` and the bare origin `https://mcp.talonic.com` as connector URLs — POST/DELETE/SSE traffic at either path routes through the same Streamable HTTP transport. Plain `GET /` still returns the discovery JSON. Either URL works in any of the install paths below.

Connect from Claude.ai's custom-connector flow. The connector authenticates against `app.talonic.com` using OAuth 2.1 with PKCE and Dynamic Client Registration, so no API key is pasted into the connector config. Once authorised, all nine Talonic tools and both resources are available to Claude.ai's agent.

items: In Claude.ai, open Settings → Connectors → Add custom connector.
Set the URL to `https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp`.
Save. Claude.ai opens a sign-in window pointing at `app.talonic.com`.
Sign in to your Talonic account (or sign up if you do not have one).
Approve the connector consent screen.
You are redirected back to Claude.ai. The connector shows `connected` and the tools light up in the conversation.
Verify with a prompt like `What is my Talonic balance?` — it should call `talonic_get_balance` and return your tier and credit count.

content.text: The connector does not need an API key in its config. Token rotation is handled by the OAuth flow.
Recommended for IDE-style clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork). Runs on your machine via stdio; requires Node.js 18 or later. Uses a `TALONIC_API_KEY` from `app.talonic.com`.
The `-y` flag skips the npm install prompt. Pinning to `@latest` means new versions are picked up on the next client restart.
Hosted with a Bearer header (custom-header clients)

For clients that drive a hosted MCP endpoint but do not support OAuth handshakes (`mcp-inspector` and similar). The hosted server at `mcp.talonic.com/mcp` accepts an API key via the `Authorization: Bearer tlnc_…` header.
All three install paths expose the same nine tools and two resources.

faq.question: How do I install the Talonic MCP server?
faq.answer: Easiest path: Claude.ai → Settings → Connectors → Add custom connector, URL https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp, sign in to app.talonic.com when prompted. No API key in config. For IDE-style clients, install locally via npx -y @talonic/mcp@latest with TALONIC_API_KEY in env.

faq.question: What is the difference between the three install paths?
faq.answer: Claude.ai OAuth connector: no API key in config, recommended for Claude.ai users. Local stdio (npx): for IDE clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork) using a TALONIC_API_KEY env var. Hosted with Bearer header: for tools like mcp-inspector that drive a hosted endpoint but do not support OAuth.

title: Authentication
description: Three ways to authenticate the Talonic MCP server: OAuth 2.1 via Claude.ai connector (no key in config), Authorization header on hosted, or env var on local stdio.

content.text: Each user runs against their own isolated Talonic workspace. Your documents and schemas are private to you. There are three authentication paths depending on how you connect.

When you connect via Claude.ai's custom-connector flow, the connector launches an OAuth 2.1 sign-in to `app.talonic.com` (PKCE + Dynamic Client Registration). After consent, the connector exchanges a short-lived bearer token that is rotated automatically; no API key sits in the connector config. The Talonic MCP server validates the token on each request against the API, so revocation propagates immediately.

items: Sign up at [https://app.talonic.com](https://app.talonic.com) if you do not already have an account. Free tier: 50 extractions per day, no credit card.
In Claude.ai, add `https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp` as a custom connector.
Sign in to Talonic when the connector prompts. Approve consent. Done.

content.text: For tools like `mcp-inspector` that drive the hosted endpoint but do not run an OAuth handshake, pass an API key in the `Authorization` header:

items: `Authorization: Bearer tlnc_...` header (preferred for header-supporting clients).
`?apiKey=tlnc_...` query parameter (only for clients that cannot set custom headers).

content.text: Set `TALONIC_API_KEY` in the `env` block of your MCP client config. The local server reads it at startup.

items: Sign up at [https://app.talonic.com](https://app.talonic.com). Free tier: 50 extractions per day, no credit card.
Settings → API Keys → Create New Key.
Copy the `tlnc_` value into the `env.TALONIC_API_KEY` of your MCP client config.

faq.question: How do I authenticate with the hosted MCP server?
faq.answer: Easiest path is the Claude.ai custom-connector flow, which runs OAuth 2.1 against app.talonic.com and rotates the bearer token automatically — no key in the connector config. For non-OAuth clients, pass an API key via the Authorization: Bearer header, or as a ?apiKey= query parameter on the URL.

title: Claude Desktop
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Claude Desktop — Talonic Docs
description: Step-by-step setup for the Talonic MCP server in Claude Desktop on macOS and Windows. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Fully restart Claude Desktop (Cmd+Q on macOS, not just close the window). Talonic appears in the connected servers list with all nine tools and two resources.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic to Claude Desktop?
faq.answer: Edit claude_desktop_config.json, add the Talonic MCP server config (hosted URL or local npx) with your API key, and fully restart Claude Desktop (Cmd+Q on macOS).

title: Cursor
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Cursor — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in Cursor IDE. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Edit `~/.cursor/mcp.json` (or open Cursor settings → MCP → edit config):

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Cursor?
faq.answer: Edit ~/.cursor/mcp.json and add the Talonic MCP server config with your API key. Hosted or local.

title: Cline
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Cline — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in the Cline VS Code extension. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Open the Cline panel → settings (gear icon) → MCP Servers → Edit.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Cline?
faq.answer: Open the Cline panel settings, go to MCP Servers, click Edit, and add the Talonic config entry.

title: Continue
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Continue — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in Continue for VS Code and JetBrains. Hosted and local configs.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Continue?
faq.answer: Edit ~/.continue/config.json and add a Talonic entry to the mcpServers array with your API key.

title: Cowork
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Cowork — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in Cowork. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Open Cowork settings → MCP Servers → Add.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Cowork?
faq.answer: Open Cowork settings, go to MCP Servers, click Add, and paste the standard Talonic config with your API key.

title: talonic_extract
description: Extract structured, schema-validated data from a document. Full input schema, output schema, confidence scores, and usage examples.

content.text: Extract structured, schema-validated data from a document. Returns clean JSON matching the schema, with per-field confidence scores and document metadata.

items: The user has a document (PDF, image, scan, DOCX) and wants specific fields pulled out.
You need structured data (vendor name, total amount, dates, parties, terms) rather than free text.
The user uploads or references any invoice, contract, certificate, statement, or form.
You want validated JSON instead of trying to OCR + parse with raw LLM calls.
The user just wants full text content → use `talonic_to_markdown`.
The user wants to find documents matching a query → use `talonic_search` or `talonic_filter`.

params.name: file_data
params.description: Base64-encoded file bytes. **Recommended for local-stdio installs** (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork), which have no size cap. Pair with `filename` for MIME type inference. **Warning for the Claude.ai hosted connector:** Claude.ai caps tool-call arguments at ~32 KB decoded, so `file_data` cannot carry a real PDF — it is silently truncated. Use `talonic_request_upload` (browser-handoff upload), `file_url`, or a pre-uploaded `document_id` instead when running through Claude.ai.

params.name: filename
params.description: Original filename with extension, e.g. `invoice.pdf`. Required when using `file_data`.

params.name: file_path
params.description: Local filesystem path. Only works if the MCP server process can read it — sandboxed clients should use `file_data` instead.

params.name: file_url
params.description: Public URL. The Talonic API fetches it server-side.

params.name: document_id
params.description: Re-extract an already-uploaded document with a new schema.

params.name: schema
params.description: Inline schema definition. **Use full JSON Schema format** (`{type: "object", properties: {...}}`) for best results. Flat key-type maps (`{vendor_name: "string"}`) are accepted but normalized server-side — if you get a 'no fields' error, switch to full JSON Schema.

params.name: schema_id
params.description: ID of a saved schema. Accepts UUID or `SCH-XXXXXXXX` short ID. Mutually exclusive with `schema`.

params.name: instructions
params.description: Natural-language guidance for the extractor, e.g. `Focus on the billing section. Amounts are in EUR.`

params.name: include_markdown
params.description: Include OCR-converted markdown alongside structured data in the response.

params.name: include_provenance
params.description: Include per-field provenance showing where each value was found: source_text (the matched text), section (document section), and page (page number).

content.text: Each field in the `confidence` object is a float from 0.0 to 1.0. Values above **0.90** are high confidence. Values between **0.70–0.90** should be treated with caution — flag them to the user for verification. Values below **0.70** indicate low confidence — the agent should ask the user to verify the value or re-extract with more specific instructions.

Specify the fields you want: provide a `schema` or `schema_id`, or set `auto_schema: true` for open capture (Talonic discovers the fields and returns a suggested schema). A call giving none of the three returns a validation error that hands back a ready-to-paste minimal schema.

Each `talonic_extract` call with a new file consumes **one extraction credit**. Re-extracting the same `document_id` with a different schema also consumes one credit. The per-call cost is surfaced on the response under `cost` (`costCredits`, `costEur`, `balanceCredits`, plus a breakdown of how many cells were resolved by the registry vs the AI), parsed from the `X-Talonic-Cost-*` and `X-Talonic-Balance-*` response headers. To avoid unnecessary cost, check if a document has already been extracted before calling again — use `talonic_search` or `talonic_filter` to find existing results, and `talonic_get_balance` to check your runway before kicking off a large batch.

params.name: unauthorized
params.description: Invalid or missing API key.

params.name: validation_error
params.description: Missing schema, invalid schema format, or no file source provided.

params.name: unsupported_file_type
params.description: MIME type inferred as `application/octet-stream`. Ensure the filename has a recognized extension (pdf, docx, png, jpg, etc.).

params.name: rate_limit_exceeded
params.description: Too many requests. The SDK retries automatically with backoff.

params.name: insufficient_credits
params.description: Extraction credit balance exhausted. Upgrade plan or wait for daily reset (free tier).

faq.question: What does talonic_extract return?
faq.answer: Structured JSON matching your schema, per-field confidence scores (0.0–1.0), document metadata (type, language, page count), and extraction/schema IDs for follow-up calls.

faq.question: How do I handle low confidence scores?
faq.answer: Scores above 0.90 are reliable. Between 0.70–0.90, flag to the user for verification. Below 0.70, ask the user to verify or re-extract with more specific instructions.

title: talonic_search
description: Omnisearch across documents, fields, sources, and schemas. Full input/output schema, examples, and usage guidance.

content.text: Omnisearch across documents, extracted field values, field names, sources, and schemas in the workspace. Returns ranked results across all entity types in one call.

items: The user wants to find documents but doesn't know the exact filename or ID.
The query is conceptual: 'contracts mentioning indemnification', 'Acme invoices'.
You need to narrow a large workspace before calling `talonic_extract` or `talonic_filter`.
The user asks 'do I have any docs about X' or 'find anything related to X'.
You need to discover canonical field names before using `talonic_filter`.
The user has a specific `document_id` → use `talonic_get_document`.
The user wants structured field-value filters like 'amount > 1000' → use `talonic_filter`.
The user wants to extract data from a new document → use `talonic_extract`.

params.name: query
params.description: Natural-language search query, e.g. `indemnification clauses` or `Acme invoices Q4`.

params.name: limit
params.description: Maximum results per entity type. Default: 5. Increase for broader exploration.

content.title: Example response

content.text: Only `fields[]` entries with `filterable: true` can be used with `talonic_filter`. These have extracted data in the workspace. Fields with `filterable: false` exist in a schema definition but have no extracted data yet — they become filterable after documents are processed against their schema.

Every `fieldMatches[]` and `fields[]` entry carries a `dataType` (`"string"`, `"number"`, `"array"`, etc.). Use it to pick the right `talonic_filter` operator on the first call — numeric operators (`gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `between`) only resolve correctly when `dataType === "number"`. See the *Schema typing* section under `talonic_filter` for the full preventive / reactive pattern.
Search calls are **free** — they do not consume extraction credits. Use search liberally to explore before extracting.

params.name: unauthorized
params.description: Invalid or missing API key.

params.name: validation_error
params.description: Empty or missing `query` parameter.

faq.question: Does talonic_search cost credits?
faq.answer: No. Search calls are free and do not consume extraction credits.

faq.question: What entities does talonic_search return?
faq.answer: Documents, field matches (with canonical names and values), sources, schemas, and field definitions — all ranked by relevance score.

faq.question: How do I avoid a `talonic_filter` numeric query returning zero matches?
faq.answer: Check `dataType` on the field entry in the search response before constructing the filter. Numeric operators (`gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `between`) only resolve against fields where `dataType === "number"`. If the type is `string` (common for monetary or formatted-number fields), suggest the user change the field's data type in the schema before filtering.

title: talonic_filter
description: Filter documents by extracted field values. Full operator reference, input/output schema, composable condition examples, and the preventive + reactive pattern for guarding numeric operators against string-typed fields.

content.text: Filter documents by extracted field values using composable conditions. Conditions accept canonical field names (e.g. `vendor.name`, `policy.0_coverage_type`) or field UUIDs. The Talonic API resolves names to IDs server-side.

items: The user wants documents matching specific structured criteria: 'invoices over 1000 EUR', 'contracts expiring before 2026-12-31', 'COIs from Acme'.
The query is value-based on extracted fields, not a free-text concept search.
You need a sortable, paginated list filtered by field conditions.
The user wants conceptual / free-text search → use `talonic_search`.
The user wants a single document by ID → use `talonic_get_document`.
The user wants to extract from a new document → use `talonic_extract`.

params.name: conditions
params.description: One or more filter conditions, AND-ed together.

params.name: search
params.description: Optional free-text search applied alongside the filters.

params.name: sort
params.description: Optional sort: `{ field, direction }` where direction is `asc` or `desc`.

params.name: page
params.description: Page number for pagination (1-based).

params.name: limit
params.description: Results per page. Default: 50.

params.name: source_connection_id
params.description: Optionally scope to a specific source connection.

params.name: field
params.description: Canonical field name (e.g. `vendor.name`). Mutually exclusive with `field_id`. Discover via `talonic_search`.

params.name: field_id
params.description: Talonic field UUID. Mutually exclusive with `field`.

params.name: operator
params.description: One of: `eq`, `neq`, `gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `between`, `contains`, `is_empty`, `is_not_empty`.

params.name: value
params.description: Value to compare against. Not needed for `is_empty` / `is_not_empty`.

params.name: value_to
params.description: Upper bound for `between` operator; ignored otherwise.

params.name: eq
params.description: Exact equality.

params.name: neq
params.description: Not equal.

params.name: gt / gte
params.description: Greater than (or equal). Works on numbers and dates.

params.name: lt / lte
params.description: Less than (or equal). Works on numbers and dates.

params.name: between
params.description: Inclusive range. Requires both `value` (lower) and `value_to` (upper).

params.name: contains
params.description: Case-insensitive substring match on string fields.

params.name: is_empty
params.description: Field has no value.

params.name: is_not_empty
params.description: Field has a materialized value. Results reflect data within seconds of extraction completing; for batch-mode documents, materialization occurs after the batch poll cycle applies results.

content.text: Numeric operators (`gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `between`) only resolve correctly when the schema field is typed as `number`. A field typed as `string` that holds numeric content (e.g. `€1,500.00`) will silently return zero matches even after extraction. There are two ways to handle this — pick the right one before constructing the call.

Call `talonic_search` first and read `dataType` on the field entry. If `dataType !== "number"`, do **not** issue a numeric operator on that field. Pick a string-friendly operator (`eq`, `contains`) or warn the user that the field needs a `data_type` change in the schema before the query can succeed. This avoids the silent-zero-matches outcome entirely.

When a numeric operator is applied to a string-typed field, the API attaches a `warnings[]` array to the filter response. Each entry has `code`, `message`, `field`/`field_id`, and a `suggestion`. The MCP tool surfaces this in `structuredContent` — agents should relay the `message` (and `suggestion`, when present) to the user rather than silently retrying.

content.title: Response with a warning

content.text: Filter calls are **free** — they query already-extracted data and do not consume extraction credits.

params.name: unauthorized
params.description: Invalid or missing API key.

params.name: no_field_match
params.description: Field name not found. Use `talonic_search` to discover canonical field names first.

params.name: validation_error
params.description: Missing conditions array, invalid operator, or missing `value` for operators that require it.

faq.question: Does talonic_filter cost credits?
faq.answer: No. Filter queries already-extracted data and is free.

faq.question: How do I find field names for filtering?
faq.answer: Call talonic_search first. Use fields[] entries where filterable is true — their canonicalName values are what you pass as the field parameter in filter conditions. Fields with filterable: false have no extracted data yet and cannot be filtered.

faq.question: Why does my `talonic_filter` query with `gt` return zero matches on a numeric-looking field?
faq.answer: The schema field is almost certainly typed as `string`, not `number`. Numeric operators against string-typed fields fall back to lexicographic comparison and silently return zero. Prevention: call `talonic_search` first and check `dataType` before issuing the filter. Recovery: the response's `warnings[]` array explains the issue and suggests a `data_type` change in the schema definition.

title: talonic_get_document
description: Fetch full metadata for a single document by ID, including processing log and link URLs.

content.text: Fetch full metadata for a single document by ID. Returns filename, page count, detected document type, language, processing log, and link URLs.

items: You need details about a specific document already extracted or uploaded.
You have a `document_id` from a previous extract or search and want more context.
You called `talonic_request_upload` and need to poll until the user has uploaded the file.
The user asks 'tell me about document X'.
The user wants full text content → use `talonic_to_markdown`.
The user wants extracted structured data → use `talonic_extract`.
The user has a file but no `document_id` yet → call `talonic_extract` first.

params.name: document_id
params.description: The Talonic document ID. Get this from a previous `talonic_extract` or `talonic_search` response.

content.text: The `status` field on the response indicates where the document is in its lifecycle. When polling after `talonic_request_upload`, wait for `status` to reach `completed` before calling `talonic_extract`.

items: `pending_upload` — document slot reserved via `talonic_request_upload`, file not yet received. Poll at ~5 s intervals. The slot expires after 15 min; past `expires_at` from the request, the user did not upload in time.
`queued` / `extracting` — file received, OCR and exhaustive extraction in progress. Keep polling.
`completed` — extraction is done. This is the state to wait for before calling `talonic_extract` with a schema or schema_id — the schema-specific LLM pass runs off the cached OCR'd text.
`uploaded` — file received but the extraction queue could not be enqueued (rare, e.g. Redis unavailable). `talonic_extract` will trigger processing inline if called.
`error` / `ocr_failed` / `extraction_failed` — terminal failures. See `processing_log` for the reason.

content.text: Free — metadata lookups do not consume extraction credits.

title: talonic_to_markdown
description: Get OCR-converted markdown for a document. Full input schema, file source options, cost guidance, and usage examples.

content.text: Get OCR-converted markdown for a document. Accepts an existing `document_id` (cheapest — one API call, no re-processing), or raw file bytes, a local path, or a URL.

items: The user wants the full text content of a document for summarisation, translation, or analysis.
A previous tool call returned a `document_id` and you want to inspect its content.
The user asks 'what does the document say' or 'summarise this PDF'.
The user has a raw PDF / scan / image and wants markdown directly without designing a schema.
The user wants specific structured fields → use `talonic_extract` with a schema.

params.name: document_id
params.description: ID of an already-ingested document. **Cheapest path** — one API call, no re-processing or credit usage.

params.name: file_data
params.description: Base64-encoded file bytes. **Recommended for local-stdio installs** (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork), which have no size cap. Pair with `filename`. Consumes one extraction credit (auto-ingests the file first). **Warning for the Claude.ai hosted connector:** Claude.ai caps tool-call arguments at ~32 KB decoded, so `file_data` cannot carry a real PDF — it is silently truncated. Use `talonic_request_upload` (browser-handoff upload), `file_url`, or a pre-uploaded `document_id` instead when running through Claude.ai.

params.name: filename
params.description: Original filename with extension. Required when using `file_data`.

params.name: file_path
params.description: Local path. Only works if the MCP server has read access.

params.name: file_url
params.description: Public URL. Talonic API fetches server-side. Consumes one extraction credit.

content.text: **Free when using `document_id`** — the document is already ingested. When passing a raw file (`file_data`, `file_path`, `file_url`), the tool auto-ingests via extract first, consuming **one extraction credit**. To avoid unnecessary cost: if you've already extracted a document, reuse the `document_id` from that response.

params.name: missing_input_source
params.description: No file source provided. Provide exactly one of `document_id`, `file_data`, `file_path`, `file_url`.

params.name: multiple_input_sources
params.description: More than one file source provided.

params.name: not_found
params.description: The `document_id` does not exist in the workspace.

params.name: unsupported_file_type
params.description: Unrecognized file extension. Use a known extension (pdf, docx, png, jpg, etc.).

faq.question: Does talonic_to_markdown cost credits?
faq.answer: Free when using document_id (already ingested). Costs one extraction credit when passing a raw file, since the file must be ingested first.

faq.question: How do I avoid paying twice for markdown?
faq.answer: If you already called talonic_extract, reuse the document_id from that response to call talonic_to_markdown for free.

title: talonic_list_schemas
description: List all saved schemas with IDs, names, descriptions, field counts, and full JSON Schema definitions.

content.text: List all saved schemas in the workspace. Returns each schema with its ID, name, description, version, field count, and full JSON Schema definition.

items: The user asks what schemas they have, or wants to see existing schemas.
Before designing a new schema, check if one already covers the use case.
The user asks to extract from a known document type — find a matching saved schema.
You need a `schema_id` for `talonic_extract`.
The user wants to extract data and provides an inline schema → call `talonic_extract` directly.

content.text: Free — listing schemas does not consume extraction credits.

title: talonic_save_schema
description: Save a schema definition to the workspace for reuse. Full input schema, definition formats, and examples.

content.text: Save a schema definition to the workspace for reuse across future extractions. Returns the saved schema with its assigned `id` (UUID) and `short_id` (`SCH-XXXXXXXX`).

items: The user asks to save a schema, store a template, or reuse the schema across docs.
You have iterated on a schema with the user and they confirmed it should be saved.
The user wants to standardise extraction across many documents of the same type.
The user just wants to extract once with an inline schema → call `talonic_extract` directly.
The user has not confirmed the schema design — avoid creating clutter.

params.name: name
params.description: Human-readable name, e.g. `Standard Invoice`.

params.name: definition
params.description: Schema definition. **Use full JSON Schema format** (`{type: "object", properties: {...}}`) for best results. Flat key-type maps are accepted but normalized server-side — if you get a 'no fields' error, switch to full JSON Schema.

params.name: description
params.description: Optional description of what this schema extracts.

content.text: Flat key-type maps are normalized to JSON Schema server-side. If you encounter a 'no fields' error, switch to full JSON Schema format. For schemas with nested objects or arrays, always use full JSON Schema.

When you call `talonic_save_schema` (or update an existing schema), the API samples the field's prior extracted values. If 80% or more of a string-typed field's values parse as numbers (with at least 5 samples), the response includes a `warnings[]` suggesting `data_type: "number"`. Heed the warning if you plan to filter on that field with numeric operators.
Free — saving a schema does not consume extraction credits.

faq.question: What schema format should I use?
faq.answer: Full JSON Schema ({type: 'object', properties: {...}}) is most reliable. Flat key-type maps ({field: 'type'}) work for simple schemas but are normalized server-side and may produce errors with complex structures.

title: talonic_get_balance
description: Read the workspace credit balance, EUR value, 30-day burn rate, projected runway, tier, and next-tier-reset timestamp.

content.text: Read the user's current Talonic credit balance, EUR value, 30-day burn rate, projected runway, tier, and next-tier-reset timestamp. Use this to make budget-aware decisions before kicking off large batches or re-extractions.

items: The user asks how many credits or how much budget they have left.
You are about to run a large or expensive operation (batch extract, re-extract many documents) and want to confirm budget headroom first.
The user asks how long their balance will last at the current rate.
The user just wants the per-call cost of a single extraction — that is already on the `talonic_extract` response under `cost`.
The user wants to top up credits — route them to the dashboard at `https://app.talonic.com`.

params.name: balance_credits
params.description: Current credit balance.

params.name: balance_eur
params.description: Current balance in EUR (rounded to two decimals).

params.name: burn_rate_30d_credits
params.description: Total credits consumed in the trailing 30 days.

params.name: projected_runway_days
params.description: Days of runway at the current 30-day average burn. `-1` indicates no consumption in the trailing window (cannot compute runway).

params.name: tier
params.description: API tier of the workspace (`free`, `pro`, `enterprise`, etc.).

params.name: tier_resets_at
params.description: ISO 8601 timestamp of the next monthly tier reset.

content.text: Free — balance lookups do not consume extraction credits.

faq.question: Does talonic_get_balance cost credits?
faq.answer: No. Balance lookups are free.

faq.question: What does projected_runway_days mean?
faq.answer: Days of runway at the trailing 30-day average burn rate. The value -1 means no consumption in the trailing window, so runway cannot be computed.

title: talonic_request_upload
description: Request a browser upload link for files too large for tool-call arguments or when running in a sandboxed hosted environment.

content.text: Request a browser upload link for the user. Use this when the user wants to extract a file but you cannot deliver it directly — the file is too large for tool-call arguments (~32 KB cap on hosted connectors), or you're running in a sandboxed environment (Claude.ai, ChatGPT) that blocks outbound file transfers.

items: The user has a file to extract but you cannot send it via `file_data` (file larger than ~32 KB, or the environment blocks outbound data).
You are running in a hosted/sandboxed environment (Claude.ai, ChatGPT) where `file_data` cannot be used reliably.
The user explicitly asks for an upload link.
You can deliver the file directly via `file_data` (local stdio installs with small files).
The file is already accessible via a public URL → use `file_url` on `talonic_extract`.
The document is already in the workspace → use `document_id` on `talonic_extract`.
Call `talonic_request_upload` with the filename. You receive a `document_id`, an `upload_url`, and an `expires_at` timestamp.
Show the `upload_url` to the user and ask them to open it in their browser.
The user drops the file on the upload page. The browser uploads directly to Talonic — no tool-call size cap, no sandbox restriction.
Poll with `talonic_get_document` using the `document_id` until `status` is `completed` (the file has been received, OCR'd, and is ready for schema-specific extraction).
Call `talonic_extract` with the `document_id` and a schema to extract structured data.

content.title: Don't trust the user's 'done' message — poll instead
content.text: When the user types 'uploaded', 'done', or 'I dropped it', that only confirms the browser-side upload completed. Server-side OCR and exhaustive extraction take another 10–30 seconds. Calling `talonic_extract` before `talonic_get_document` reports `status: 'completed'` may return errors. Always poll, regardless of what the user says.

params.name: filename
params.description: The name of the file being uploaded, including extension (e.g. `invoice.pdf`). Used to pre-allocate the document and infer MIME type.

params.name: document_id
params.description: The pre-allocated document ID. Use with `talonic_get_document` to poll status, and with `talonic_extract` once uploaded.

params.name: upload_url
params.description: URL the user should open in their browser to drop the file. Expires after 15 minutes.

params.name: expires_at
params.description: ISO 8601 timestamp when the upload link expires.

content.text: Upload links are single-use and expire after 15 minutes. If the user doesn't upload in time, call `talonic_request_upload` again to get a fresh link.

faq.question: How do I upload a file through Claude.ai to Talonic?
faq.answer: Call talonic_request_upload with the filename. Show the returned upload_url to the user. They open it in their browser and drop the file. Poll talonic_get_document until status is 'completed' (the document has been received and processed), then call talonic_extract with the document_id and a schema.

faq.question: Why can't I just send the file through file_data on Claude.ai?
faq.answer: Claude.ai's hosted connector caps tool-call arguments at ~32 KB (decoded). Real documents are typically 100 KB to several MB. The browser-handoff upload bypasses this limit entirely by moving the file transfer to the user's browser.

title: Resources
description: Two MCP resources exposed by the Talonic server: talonic://schemas (saved schemas) and talonic://webhooks/reference (webhook event reference).

content.text: Exposes the saved-schemas list to clients that browse MCP resources separately. Claude Desktop and Cowork render these in the UI. The contents mirror `talonic_list_schemas` but in a browseable form.

Static reference documenting the webhook events the Talonic API can fire (extraction lifecycle, document classification, etc.), their payload shapes, and how to subscribe. Useful when an agent is helping the user wire Talonic into a backend that needs to react to extraction events.

faq.question: What resources does the Talonic MCP server expose?
faq.answer: Two resources: talonic://schemas (browseable list of saved schemas) and talonic://webhooks/reference (static reference for the API's webhook events and payloads).

title: Cost & Rate Limits
seoTitle: MCP Cost and Rate Limits — Talonic Docs
description: Which MCP tool calls cost extraction credits, rate limit behavior, insufficient-credit handling, and how to avoid re-extraction.

content.text: Only extraction operations consume credits. Everything else is free:

params.name: talonic_extract
params.description: Each call with a new file or re-extraction of an existing document.

params.name: talonic_to_markdown (raw file)
params.description: When passing `file_data`, `file_path`, or `file_url` — auto-ingests first.

params.name: talonic_to_markdown (document_id)
params.description: Document already ingested — just fetches stored markdown.

params.name: talonic_search
params.description: Queries indexed data.

params.name: talonic_filter
params.description: Queries extracted field values.

params.name: talonic_get_document
params.description: Metadata lookup.

params.name: talonic_list_schemas
params.description: Lists saved schemas.

params.name: talonic_save_schema
params.description: Persists a schema definition.

params.name: talonic_get_balance
params.description: Reads workspace credit balance, EUR value, burn rate, runway, tier.

content.text: The per-call cost of `talonic_extract` (and `talonic_to_markdown` with a raw file) is also surfaced on the response under `cost` (`costCredits`, `costEur`, `balanceCredits`, plus a breakdown of `cellsResolvedRegistry` and `cellsResolvedAi`). Agents can read this immediately after the call rather than calling `talonic_get_balance` again.
Agents should avoid extracting the same document twice. Best practices:

items: After calling `talonic_extract`, save the returned `document.id` and `extraction.id`. Use these for follow-up calls (`talonic_to_markdown`, `talonic_get_document`) instead of re-uploading the file.
Before extracting, call `talonic_search` to check if the document already exists in the workspace.
Use `talonic_filter` to query already-extracted data instead of re-extracting with a different schema when the fields you need are already captured.

content.text: The Talonic API enforces per-key rate limits. When exceeded, the server returns `429 Too Many Requests` with a `X-RateLimit-Reset` header. The MCP server (and the underlying Node SDK) retries automatically with exponential backoff up to `maxRetries` (default: 3). If retries are exhausted, the tool returns an error with the reset timestamp.

When extraction credits are exhausted, the API returns `402 Payment Required`. The tool surfaces this as an error. The agent should inform the user that their credit balance is depleted and suggest upgrading their plan or waiting for the daily reset (free tier: 50 extractions/day, resets at midnight UTC).
The free tier includes 50 extractions per day (resets at midnight UTC). Search, filter, metadata, and schema operations are unlimited. No credit card required.

faq.question: Which MCP tool calls cost credits?
faq.answer: Only talonic_extract and talonic_to_markdown (with raw file) cost one credit each. Search, filter, get_document, list_schemas, and save_schema are all free.

faq.question: What happens when I run out of credits?
faq.answer: The API returns 402 Payment Required. The agent should inform the user and suggest upgrading their plan or waiting for the daily reset (free tier resets at midnight UTC).

faq.question: How do rate limits work?
faq.answer: The API returns 429 with a reset timestamp. The SDK retries automatically with exponential backoff (up to 3 retries by default).

title: Drag & Drop in Chat
seoTitle: MCP Drag and Drop File Handling — Talonic Docs
description: How file uploads work in MCP chat clients: file_data + filename in local stdio installs, file_url or document_id when using the Claude.ai hosted connector.

content.text: When a user drag-drops a file into a chat-style MCP host, the file lands in a host-owned sandbox directory the MCP server cannot read. The path the host hands the agent is meaningless to a separately-running `npx` MCP process. Two different paths solve this, depending on whether you're on a local stdio install or the Claude.ai hosted connector.
Local stdio installs (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork)

From `@talonic/mcp@0.1.4`, agents can pass **`file_data`** (base64-encoded file bytes) and **`filename`** on `talonic_extract` and `talonic_to_markdown`. The agent reads the file bytes from the conversation, base64-encodes them, and passes them through the MCP tool call. The MCP server decodes, infers MIME type from the filename, and uploads to the Talonic API as a normal multipart request. Tool descriptions advertise `file_data` as the recommended input here, so well-trained agents reach for it automatically. No client-side configuration required.

Claude.ai's hosted-connector pipeline imposes a hard size limit on tool-call arguments (effectively ~32 KB decoded). A base64-encoded real PDF (typically hundreds of KB at minimum) gets truncated before reaching the MCP server. The Talonic API receives ~32 KB, registers an empty document, and the response comes back with `null` extracted fields. This is a Claude.ai platform limit on connectors, not a Talonic MCP server bug.

items: `talonic_request_upload`: the recommended path. The agent gets an upload link, the user drops the file in their browser, and the agent continues with the `document_id`. Works with any file size the API accepts.
`file_url`: pass a publicly reachable URL; the Talonic API fetches it server-side. Best for files already on the public web.
`document_id`: upload the file once via `app.talonic.com`, then reference the returned id. Best for sensitive files you don't want to expose publicly.
Switch to a local stdio install (`npx -y @talonic/mcp@latest` in Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, etc.) — local stdio has no parameter-size cap and `file_data` works for any file size the API accepts.

content.text: From `@talonic/mcp@0.1.7`, this is solved by the **`talonic_request_upload`** tool. The agent calls it to get a one-time upload URL plus a reserved `document_id`. The user opens the link in their browser and drops the file — no tool-call size cap, no sandbox restriction. The agent then polls `talonic_get_document` until the file is ready and proceeds with `talonic_extract` using the `document_id`.

faq.question: How does file upload work in MCP chat clients?
faq.answer: In local stdio installs (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork), agents pass file_data (base64) + filename and the MCP server uploads to Talonic. In the Claude.ai hosted connector, file_data is truncated by a platform-level argument-size cap, so use file_url or document_id instead.

faq.question: Why does drag-and-drop fail on the Claude.ai connector?
faq.answer: Claude.ai imposes a ~32 KB cap on tool-call argument values. A base64-encoded real PDF cannot fit. Use talonic_request_upload to get a browser upload link — the user drops the file in their browser, bypassing the cap entirely. Alternatives: file_url (public URL), document_id (upload via app.talonic.com first), or switch to a local stdio install.

title: Architecture
description: How the Talonic MCP server connects agents to the Talonic API. Hosted (HTTP) and local (stdio) transport modes.

content.text: The MCP server sits between the AI agent and the Talonic API:

content.title: Architecture
content.code:
Agent (Claude Desktop / Cursor / Cline / etc.)
  ↓ MCP protocol (stdio or HTTP)
Talonic MCP server
  ↓ HTTPS, Bearer auth
api.talonic.com

content.text: **Local mode (stdio):** The MCP client spawns the server as a child process. Communication is via stdin/stdout using the MCP protocol.

**Hosted mode (HTTP):** The server runs at `mcp.talonic.com` using Streamable HTTP transport. Each `initialize` request creates a new session keyed by a UUID (`Mcp-Session-Id` header). Subsequent requests route to the same session. If a session is lost (deploy, restart), the server returns 404 and the client re-initializes — standard MCP reconnection.

Each tool call is one HTTP request to the Talonic API using your API key. The server handles auth, retries on transient failures (429, 5xx), MIME-type detection on file uploads, multipart serialisation, and structured error formatting.

title: Configuration
description: Environment variables for the local MCP server and header options for the hosted server.

content.text: Set via the `env` block in your MCP client config:

params.name: TALONIC_API_KEY
params.description: Your Talonic API key. Starts with `tlnc_`.

params.name: TALONIC_BASE_URL
params.description: Override the API base URL. Default: `https://api.talonic.com`.

content.text: The hosted server at `mcp.talonic.com` is configured entirely via the MCP client config:
No environment variables needed. The API key is passed via the `Authorization` header on every request.

title: Troubleshooting
description: Common issues and fixes for the Talonic MCP server: missing API key, invisible server, 500 errors, and filter problems.

content.text: The `env` block in your MCP client config is missing or not being read. Double-check the JSON shape. After editing, fully restart the client (not just the conversation).
The `Authorization` header is missing or malformed. Ensure the config has `"headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer tlnc_..." }`. The key must start with `tlnc_`.
Talonic does not appear in connected servers

For local: make sure the `command` is `npx` and `args` are exactly `["-y", "@talonic/mcp@latest"]`. Sanity check: run `npx -y @talonic/mcp@latest --version` in a terminal. For hosted: ensure the `url` is `https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp` (note the `/mcp` path).
talonic_extract returns a validation error when no schema is given

By design, to keep results reliable. The error hands back a ready-to-paste minimal JSON Schema (tailored to your `instructions`) so the retry succeeds immediately. Three ways forward: provide an inline `schema` (full JSON Schema recommended), pass a `schema_id` from `talonic_list_schemas`, or set `auto_schema: true` for open capture — Talonic discovers the fields and returns a suggested schema you can refine.
Claude.ai connector: stuck on sign-in or callback

The Claude.ai custom-connector flow runs OAuth 2.1 with PKCE against `app.talonic.com`. If the sign-in window opens but does not return to Claude.ai, or the connector reports `disconnected` after authorising: confirm you are signed in to the same Talonic account in `app.talonic.com` that owns the workspace you want to use; allow third-party cookies for `app.talonic.com` and `claude.ai` (some browsers block the post-consent redirect otherwise); after granting consent you should be redirected back to Claude.ai automatically — if the tab stays on `app.talonic.com`, close the tab and reopen the connector to retry; if you have multiple Talonic accounts, sign out of `app.talonic.com` first; as a last resort, remove the connector in Claude.ai and add it again with `https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp` as the URL.
The MIME type was inferred as `application/octet-stream`. Ensure the filename has a recognized extension (pdf, docx, png, jpg, etc.).
Field names must be canonical names from the field registry (e.g. `vendor.name`). Call `talonic_search` first; canonical names appear in `fields[].canonicalName`.
Extraction credit balance is exhausted. Free tier: 50 extractions/day, resets at midnight UTC. Upgrade plan or wait for reset.
Some MCP clients cache tool descriptions. Restart the client after a server update.

Sessions are in-memory on the hosted server. After a deploy or restart, existing sessions are lost. The client should re-initialize without the `Mcp-Session-Id` header — most MCP clients handle this automatically.
